name: Villamanín from La Robla - circular by Torío river and La Profunda Mine
name_original: Villamanín desde La Robla – circular por río Torío y Mina La Profunda
region_intro: Road cycling routes around La Robla traverse a diverse landscape within the province of León, characterized by mountainous terrain, deep valleys, and significant natural features. The region offers varied elevation gains, with routes winding through areas like the Valle de Gordón and the Bernesga Valley. Cyclists encounter limestone gorges, such as Vegacervera Gorge, and can explore natural attractions like the Ciñera Beech Forest. This varied topography provides a range of road cycling experiences. Best road cycling routes around La Robla The most popular road cycling route is Barrios de Luna Reservoir and La Robla Loop, a 46.8 miles (75.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 48 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain and passes by the Barrios de Luna Reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Lorenzana from La Robla - circular via Tapia de la Ribera and Selga de Ordás Reservoir, a moderate 35.9 miles (57.9 km) path. This route offers views of the Selga de Ordás Reservoir and the surrounding rural landscape. Local road cyclists also love the Villamanín from La Robla - circular by Torío river and La Profunda Mine, a 36.3 miles (58.4 km) trail leading through the Torío river valley, often completed in about 2 hours 47 minutes. Road cycling around La Robla is defined by mountainous terrain, deep valleys, and limestone gorges. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult routes. The routes in La Robla are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 4 reviews. More than 800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore La Robla's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around La Robla?
A: La Robla offers a wide selection of road cycling routes, with over 20 options available. These routes cater to various skill levels, from moderate to difficult, ensuring there's a suitable ride for most cyclists.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in La Robla?
A: While many routes in La Robla present moderate to difficult challenges due to the mountainous terrain, there are some easier options available. For instance, routes with less elevation gain can be more suitable for those new to road cycling or looking for a more relaxed ride.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while road cycling in La Robla?
A: Road cycling in La Robla offers diverse and appealing landscapes. You'll traverse mountainous terrain, deep valleys like the Valle de Gordón and Bernesga Valley, and impressive limestone gorges such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3541942" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vegacervera Gorge&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4073598" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Calderones Gorge&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features natural attractions like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5235085" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ciñera Beech Forest&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Absolutely. La Robla is known for its challenging routes, with 8 classified as difficult. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042316978/barrios-de-luna-reservoir-and-la-robla-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Barrios de Luna Reservoir and La Robla Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 46.8-mile (75.2 km) route featuring significant elevation gain, perfect for experienced cyclists seeking a demanding ride.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions to see along the cycling routes?
A: Beyond the stunning gorges and valleys, you can visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5235085" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ciñera Beech Forest&lt;/a&gt;, recognized as one of Spain's best forests. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4073598" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Calderones Gorge&lt;/a&gt; is also home to the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5558242" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Virgin of Manadero in the Cave of the Doves&lt;/a&gt;, a unique rock sanctuary.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available in La Robla?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in La Robla are designed as circular loops. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1046681315/lorenzana-from-la-robla-circular-via-tapia-de-la-ribera-and-selga-de-ordas-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lorenzana from La Robla - circular via Tapia de la Ribera and Selga de Ordás Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, which offers views of the reservoir and rural landscapes, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042300095/villamanin-from-la-robla-circular-by-torio-river-and-la-profunda-mine" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villamanín from La Robla - circular by Torío river and La Profunda Mine&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in La Robla?
A: The road cycling routes in La Robla are highly rated by the komoot community, holding an average score of 5.0 stars from 4 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the stunning natural beauty, and the rewarding challenges offered by the region's mountainous paths.

Q: Can I find routes that pass by reservoirs or significant water features?
A: Yes, several routes incorporate significant water features. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042316978/barrios-de-luna-reservoir-and-la-robla-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Barrios de Luna Reservoir and La Robla Loop&lt;/a&gt; passes directly by the Barrios de Luna Reservoir, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1046681315/lorenzana-from-la-robla-circular-via-tapia-de-la-ribera-and-selga-de-ordas-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lorenzana from La Robla - circular via Tapia de la Ribera and Selga de Ordás Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; offers views of the Selga de Ordás Reservoir.

Q: Are there any routes that explore the region's mining heritage?
A: Yes, the region's mining history is visible along some routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042300095/villamanin-from-la-robla-circular-by-torio-river-and-la-profunda-mine" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villamanín from La Robla - circular by Torío river and La Profunda Mine&lt;/a&gt; leads through the Torío river valley and past the La Profunda Mine, offering a glimpse into this aspect of the area's past.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in La Robla?
A: La Robla's diverse landscape makes it appealing for road cycling through much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. While specific winter conditions can vary, the region's mountainous character suggests that summer and shoulder seasons are ideal for enjoying the full range of routes.

Q: Are there any viewpoints or high passes offering panoramic views?
A: Yes, the region provides several opportunities for expansive views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6807331" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Muria mountain pass&lt;/a&gt; is noted for its panoramic landscapes, giving cyclists the sensation of being suspended between the valley and the mountains. Additionally, viewpoints overlooking the Bernesga Valley offer picturesque vistas.
tips: The landscape of La Robla has been marked for decades by its cement plant and its thermal power station. The first continues to operate at full capacity after a reform in 2004 that made it one of the most modern facilities of this type in Europe. The second was closed in 2020 as part of the closure of all coal-fired power plants in Spain, its two cooling towers being demolished in 2022 and radically changing the environment again, just as they did when they were built.
Valdepiélago is a small town next to the Curueño river and the entrance to the Curueño gorge. Spectacular views of the jagged edge mountain rock formation as the road angles along the river gorge.
Las Hoces de Vegacervera is a protected natural space in the north of León. 12 kilometer path towards Matallana on the banks of the Torío river with spectacular panoramic views of the river gorge.
Villamanín is a small town in the La Tercia region on the N-630 national highway. Near the source of the Bernesga river on the valley route between León and Gijón.
